paragraphs.body: Silent Stars, Silenced Voices: The Dimming Light of Education for Afghan Women

I read an article about the life cycle of stars. As I was reading, it felt familiar to a story I knew. I felt like I could relate to the loneliness and pain of the star as it fades away. Once so bright and majestic, it turns into something like a black hole. In the article “Stellar Evolution,” the author explains that stars initially form from a cloud of gas and dust called a nebula and go through various phases until they die.

The phases of a small star include the main sequence, red giant, planetary nebula, white dwarf, and finally, black dwarf. Each of these phases reminded me of the happiness and struggles my generation faces in my country. The stars share the same destiny as Afghan girls. Once, they too arose from nothing, were burning brightly, and are now fading away. This is just one of a million stories. It is the story of Afghan girls.

annotations.text: The stars share the same destiny as Afghan girls. Once, they too arose from nothing, were burning brightly, and are now fading away. This is just one of a million stories. It is the story of Afghan girls.

paragraphs.body: I was born in a country that wasn’t perfect, yet I was incredibly fortunate to have a family I’ll forever cherish. From the very beginning, the women in my family have been my inspiration. My mother, the sweetest soul I know, never made me feel less than my brothers. Although she couldn’t attend school herself, she ensured I had every opportunity to grow and learn. My sisters, true role models in every sense, broke barriers, defied societal expectations, and made it possible for me to follow my dreams with ease. I vividly remember the days when my sister would sit me down and explain complex biology concepts. Though I didn’t always understand the terms, I was captivated by the projects she undertook, like the time she dissected a turtle to learn its anatomy. I still recall the smell of the chemicals and the feel of the cold tools. That moment ignited a passion for biology that has only grown stronger over time. And then there are my father and brothers. In a country like Afghanistan, they had the power to stifle my dreams and treat me as insignificant. But they didn’t. They treated me as an equal, and for that, I am forever grateful. My brother, the pride I see in his eyes when I succeed, is one of the sweetest rewards of my life. Truly, my family is my greatest treasure.

annotations.context_left: en I succeed, is one of the sweetest rewards of my life. Truly, my family is my greatest treasure.

paragraphs.body: I was living the life I had always dreamed of—shining like a star, with everything in place to achieve my goals.

One moment in class has always stuck with me. My teacher said, "If you want to change the destiny of your country, then study and change. … Do your part, and don’t let future generations be ashamed of you or struggle with the mistakes you leave behind. Just like you struggle now." To many, it might sound like a teacher giving typical motivation, but to me, it resonated deeply. That moment ignited something within me. I wasn’t just going to learn for myself; I was ready to fight, and my only weapons were a pen and a book.

annotations.text: That moment ignited something within me. I wasn’t just going to learn for myself; I was ready to fight, and my only weapons were a pen and a book.
annotations.comment: This is a great revision--the ways you're applying your ideas to your own life are powerful and resonant.

paragraphs.body: Until one day… I was in 11th grade when our midterm exams arrived. The news of other provinces falling to the Taliban was everywhere, but no one wanted to believe that Kabul could truly fall. I remember that day so clearly. I went to school for the physics midterm exam. After finishing, I returned home and started reviewing for the biology exam. I sat in my room, looking at my biology notebook, which was filled with notes, diagrams, and scribbles from countless hours of study. The room was quiet, far removed from the noise outside. The night was dark and still, as if nothing had changed, as though it was just another typical evening.

I was trying to memorize every last detail for the exam, the frantic rustling of my pages the only sound breaking the silence. Outside, I could hear my little nephew playing in the living room, his laughter a distant reminder of a simpler time. My notebook felt heavy and thick with all the knowledge I hoped to retain. I paced back and forth, trying to focus, but my mind was racing. The summer heat made the air feel stifling and dry, and my throat was parched as if I hadn't drunk water all day. My mom was cooking dinner, and the scent of rice filled the house.

annotations.text: My mom was cooking dinner, and the scent of rice filled the house.
My notebook felt heavy and thick with all the knowledge I hoped to retain.

paragraphs.body: Then, suddenly, my sister came into the room, and her words shattered the stillness: "They’ve announced that girls’ schools past sixth grade are banned." At first, I thought she was joking, but the look on her face told me she wasn’t.

annotations.text: "They’ve announced that girls’ schools past sixth grade are banned."
annotations.comment: Wow. The way you're writing is making me feel the weight, the pain, the loss. Nice work.

paragraphs.body: I felt like my whole body was shutting down. The weight in my chest grew heavier. At that moment, the vision of becoming a doctor blurred into a suffocating haze, replaced by the image of a hopeless girl, lost and waiting for help. Thousands of thoughts rushed through my mind like a storm: What is going to happen? Will I be nothing more than a woman confined to raising children? What about the imaginary surgeries I had once dreamed of, where I held the scalpel with steady hands? Yes, they were just fantasies, but in those moments, they were my future — my escape. Now, they felt distant, like a fading dream slipping through my fingers. It felt like the end of everything I had known.
The quiet that had once felt peaceful now felt suffocating.

Like the dying stars in the cosmos. Like the darkness of space where stars are born and die in quiet. In the article “Stellar Evolution,” the writer explains how the star reaches the end of its life. According to the writer, “When the hydrogen in the center of the star depletes, the star draws upon the hydrogen further from its core. This process causes the star to expand and become a red giant. …. Then, it creates a planetary nebula, and the outer layer dissipates; the star is reduced to a white dwarf, no longer shining as brightly as it once did, signaling the end of its life.” In other words, the writer is explaining the process that causes the star to expand and eventually become a dwarf. This mirrors my journey, our journey. Like the star, we once shone brightly, full of potential, but suddenly, we found ourselves resigned to a fate we never desired.

annotations.text: Like the dying stars in the cosmos. Like the darkness of space where stars are born and die in quiet.
annotations.comment: Excellent job returning to your summary and resource. Great metaphor :) Nice quote burger templates, too.

paragraphs.body: I no longer resemble that dying star, but many girls in my country still do. They remain in the dark, with their potential overlooked and their dreams restricted. I know my journey is not the reality for many others. Just one opportunity—just one chance for education— can completely change the course of a girl’s life. I’ve had that chance, and I don’t take it for granted. Yet so many girls are still waiting for theirs. The impact of education is clear, and it’s time we ensure every girl has the chance to thrive. That future can be theirs, too.

annotations.comment: Beautiful revision! Wow :). Your details are awesome; your voice is strong; your central focusing idea is clear. I'm very taken with the metaphor you create to tie it all together, too. This is a great essay :) Thanks for sharing it with me. See my comments in-text as well!
